questions.question: At 6 NM, a Diamond Twin Star is beginning the descent and will maintain a final approach ground speed of 110 knots. At 3 NM, a Piper Warrior is maintaining a ground speed of 75 knots. How far out on final will the Twin Star be when the Warrior crosses the threshold?

Two aircraft are on a collision course and will impact without intervention in the next 30 seconds. Aircraft A is flying eastbound at 270 MPH in a descent of 2,100 FPM. Aircraft B is flying westbound at 120 MPH and climbing at 1,500 FPM. What is the precise distance between the two aircraft?
If Aircraft A abruptly levels off after 10 seconds of closure, how much separation would be created when both aircraft arrive at the original predicted impact point in time?
questions.context: Referenced question 17: Two aircraft are on a collision course and will impact without intervention in the next 30 seconds. Aircraft A is flying eastbound at 270 MPH in a descent of 2,100 FPM. Aircraft B is flying westbound at 120 MPH and climbing at 1,500 FPM. What is the precise distance between the two aircraft?

questions.question: At 7 NM, an Airbus A321 is beginning the descent and will maintain a final approach ground speed of 148 knots. At 3 NM, a Piper Seneca is maintaining a ground speed of 110 knots. How far out on final will the Airbus be when the Seneca crosses the threshold?

The NIFA Metering judge needs a minimum of 9 minutes of separation between aircraft on a Navigation Event route. If a course is 72 NM long and an aircraft planning 120 KTAS is flying behind an aircraft planning 85 KTAS, how many seconds apart do the departure times need to be to have that separation the whole flight?

An aircraft is passing over you at FL340, flying a true course of 139° at 0.78M. You are traveling in the opposite direction at FL330 and 0.83M. The wind is 290@94 kts, and temperature is ISA. What is your closure rate with the other aircraft?
You and your friend depart an airport at the same time. If you are traveling at 170 MPH and your friend is doing 126 KTS how long will it take you to get 1 NM ahead of them?

Airplane A has a TAS of 120 knots and Airplane B has a TAS of 140 knots, both at 8,000'. If Airplane A departs Regional Airport 25 minutes prior to Airplane B, how long will it take for Airplane B to catch up and pass Airplane A? (Assume calm wind)

choices.text: Airplane B will not catch Airplane A

questions.question: An aircraft departs at 1510Z traveling at 85 kts. At 1530Z a second aircraft departs in the same direction at 120 kts. How long will it take the second aircraft to catch the first?

You and your friend decided to do a little flying over summer vacation, so you went back to your local airport to rent the Piper Cub. Your friend rents a C-172, and you decided to race to your lunch stop at an airport 57 nautical miles away. The course to the airport is 165°, and the average winds are 290° at 22 knots. You know the Cub isn't quite as fast, so you get a head start over your friend. If your friend in the C-172 takes off 20 minutes after you in the Piper Cub, who wins the race? The average TAS for the Cub is 75 mph, and for the C-172 it is 110 knots.

choices.text: Your skills are so equal, it's a tie.
The Cub barely wins, arriving 1 minute earlier.
A strong victory for the legendary Cub, beating the Cessna by 3 minutes.
Even that head start didn't help, the Cessna arrived 2 minutes earlier.

questions.correctChoice: A strong victory for the legendary Cub, beating the Cessna by 3 minutes.

questions.question: ATC is trying to keep 10 NM spacing between aircraft on approach. Aircraft ABC is currently spaced perfectly with preceding aircraft and is maintaining that 10 NM separation at 200 KTAS. ABC is currently 13 NM from the fix NIFAH. Aircraft XYZ is 14 NM behind ABC. If XYZ is maintaining his max speed of 250 KTAS, where will they be relative to 10 NM in trail when ABC reaches NIFAH?

Regardless of the previous answer, ATC decides to slow XYZ down to make room for departures instead. ATC wants XYZ to be at least 15 NM in trail of ABC at NIFAH. What is the max true airspeed XYZ can do and still have the needed spacing? (assume speed reduction is immediate)
questions.context: Referenced question 3: ATC is trying to keep 10 NM spacing between aircraft on approach. Aircraft ABC is currently spaced perfectly with preceding aircraft and is maintaining that 10 NM separation at 200 KTAS. ABC is currently 13 NM from the fix NIFAH. Aircraft XYZ is 14 NM behind ABC. If XYZ is maintaining his max speed of 250 KTAS, where will they be relative to 10 NM in trail when ABC reaches NIFAH?

questions.question: An air traffic controller sees two aircraft on his radar scope flying the same jet route at the same altitude 5 NM apart. He has 0:16:40 to get 30 NM separation between the two aircraft. The first jet is told to maintain max forward speed, which the controller sees from his scope is 13 NM every 2 minutes. Winds at altitude are a 60 knot headwind and temperature is -42°C. What maximum speed can the controller tell the second, trailing plane to fly in order to get 30 NM separation? Assume the time and speed for the second plane to slow down is negligible.

ATC instructs you to look out for traffic at your 12 o'clock and 5 miles. The traffic is moving in the same direction you are, but you travel 28 knots faster. Five minutes later, ATC asks if you have him in sight. How many miles away are you from the traffic?
You are standing at the threshold of runway 16 as a 747 flies overhead. At an approach speed of 152 knots how long does it take the 231-ft long plane to pass by you?

Your old flight instructor, Wings McGee takes off from the local airport in his old Skymaster. He cruises at 287 kph and departed 27 minutes ago. You depart from the local airport in your Pilatus PC-12 and cruise at 300 mph. How many nautical miles will it take before you pass your old CFI?

You are running from Clay Matthews at 1800 feet per minute, and he is running at 12.2 meters per second. Yeah he runs fast. If he will tackle you when he catches you in 2 seconds, how far did you have to run?

Jerry is flying his single engine airplane home at 138 knots groundspeed on a true course of 225° when he accidentally flys into a TFR. At that moment, with 92 nm remaining on his flight, a military jet is sent to intercept him from a base that is to the northeast (specifically 045°) and 198 nm away. If the jet flies at 465 KTAS, how long will it take for the jet to intercept Jerry in his single engine airplane? Wind is calm.
How far from home is the single engine airplane intercepted?
questions.context: Referenced question 18: Jerry is flying his single engine airplane home at 138 knots groundspeed on a true course of 225° when he accidentally flys into a TFR. At that moment, with 92 nm remaining on his flight, a military jet is sent to intercept him from a base that is to the northeast (specifically 045°) and 198 nm away. If the jet flies at 465 KTAS, how long will it take for the jet to intercept Jerry in his single engine airplane? Wind is calm.

questions.question: The controller needs to assure that as the first aircraft arrives at the runway, the second aircraft is 5 statute miles behind so that he can make room for a departure. If the second aircraft is assigned a final approach speed of 150 knots when he is 6.5 statute miles behind the first aircraft and 12.5 statute miles from the airport, will he have adequate spacing to meet the 5-statute mile criteria?

choices.text: No, the first aircraft will reach the runway with only 2 statute miles of spacing
No, the second aircraft would need to fly 300 km per hour to ensure spacing
Yes, the first aircraft will land 6.2 statute miles ahead of the second.
Yes, the first aircraft will land 5.3 statute miles ahead of the second.

questions.correctChoice: Yes, the first aircraft will land 5.3 statute miles ahead of the second.

questions.question: You and your buddy Roy are each taking an airplane for a trip to get some lunch at an airport 230 NM away. What a perfect opportunity to test the range of your new pocket-size emergency transceiver. You have a TAS of 108 knots and Roy's TAS is 161 knots. The useable range for your transceiver is listed at 35 NM. Assuming you both leave the airport at the same time, how far will you fly before you should exceed the transceiver's range? (Assume calm wind)
How far was Roy from the destination when you actually lost contact with him?
questions.context: Referenced question 19: You and your buddy Roy are each taking an airplane for a trip to get some lunch at an airport 230 NM away. What a perfect opportunity to test the range of your new pocket-size emergency transceiver. You have a TAS of 108 knots and Roy's TAS is 161 knots. The useable range for your transceiver is listed at 35 NM. Assuming you both leave the airport at the same time, how far will you fly before you should exceed the transceiver's range? (Assume calm wind) [[DIVIDER]] (Refer to previous problem) It turns out that you and Roy were actually 40 NM apart before contact was lost.

questions.question: Aircraft Alpha is trying to catch up to and pass aircraft Bravo. Alpha is flying at Mach 1.63 while Bravo is flying at Mach 0.78. OAT is -38°C. When the chase begins the airplanes are 65 NM apart. How long would it take for Alpha to be 37 SM ahead of Bravo?
